answer:
**1. 5 Highest-Converting Cold DM/Outbound Opener Structures for 2025-2026**
The sources highlight five specific high-converting cold DM/outreach structures targeted at B2B leaders, founders, and CEOs:

*   **The Passive DM / Google Alerts Inbound Loop:** Triggers off a recent company milestone with a "zero-ask" message to a founder or CEO [1, 2].
    *   *Template:* “Congrats on the recent round, {{FirstName}}. I'm a healthcare technology veteran (ZocDoc/PatientPop) and love what you're doing. Following you all from way over here in Los Angeles.” [1, 3]
*   **The Specific Signal Opener:** Ties a recent event directly to a problem you solve and provides named social proof [4]. 
    *   *Template:* "Hi {{first name}}, Saw {{specific trigger — new hire, funding round, product launch, podcast appearance}}. Congrats on that. {{One sentence connecting the trigger to a problem you solve}}. We've helped {{2 named similar companies}} cut {{specific metric}} by {{specific number}}. Worth a 12-minute call next Tuesday to see if it lines up?" [4]
*   **The Trigger-Led InMail:** Offers upfront value based on a recent event, proposing to send a resource rather than demanding a meeting [5].
    *   *Template:* "Hi {{first name}}, Saw {{trigger event}}, that usually means {{specific implication for their role}} is on your plate. We've helped {{comparable company}} solve {{specific problem}} in about {{timeframe}}. Happy to share the playbook. no demo needed. Want me to send it over?" [5]
*   **The No-Pitch Connection Request:** Bypasses the “sales pitch” filter by referencing real profile activity and explicitly promising no pitch [6].
    *   *Template:* "Hi {{first name}}, came across your post on {{topic}}, the point about {{specific detail}} matched what I'm seeing with {{similar company type}} teams. Would be useful to be connected. No pitch." [6]
*   **The Post-Connection Genuine Question DM:** Sent after a connection is accepted, asking an open-ended operational question without a call-to-action [5].
    *   *Template:* "Thanks for connecting, {{first name}}. Genuine question: how is your team handling {{specific operational problem}} right now? I keep running into {{similar role}} who are doing X but say Y is still broken. Curious where you've landed." [5]

**2. Documented Reply Rates (Percentages)**
*   **(a) Generic templated openers:** 1.7% reply rate for cold emails [7, 8]. For generic broadcast Instagram DMs, the conversion rate rarely exceeds 5% [9].
*   **(b) Permission-based openers:** 11.18% success rate (Note: This is documented for *cold calls*, asking for 27 to 30 seconds upfront; it is not documented for DMs in the sources) [10, 11]. 
*   **(c) Anti-pitch/reverse openers:** **Not in sources.** Exact percentages for this are not provided in the text, though it is noted they yield "High conversion rates with highly skeptical, senior buyers" on cold calls [11].
*   **(d) Pattern-interrupt openers:** (Note: Documented for *cold calls* only, not DMs). The "Honesty interrupt" has a 30% retention rate past 30 seconds; "How have you been?" has a 10.01% meeting-booking rate; and the odd-number time request has an 11.18% success rate [11].
*   **(e) Loom video DMs:** **Not in sources.**

**3. Specific Words and Phrases to Avoid**
The exact phrase *'AI cadence smell'* is **not in sources**. However, the sources explicitly recommend avoiding the following words and phrases in cold outreach because they trigger spam filters, sound robotic, or immediately flag you as a junior/generic salesperson:
*   "Did I catch you at a bad time?" [12]
*   "How are you today?" [13]
*   "I'm just calling to check in," "I wanted to follow up," or "Just want to circle back" [14, 15]
*   "Are you the decision-maker?" or "Do you have budget for this?" [15, 16]
*   "I know you're busy, but..." [15]
*   "Let me know..." or "I'd be happy to..." [15]
*   "Does that make sense?" or "Do you know what we do?" [15]
*   "We are the #1 provider..." [15]
*   "Free", "Act now", "Guaranteed", "Free money", "Click here", or "Limited time offer" [17-19]
*   "Love your content" or "Love the shit you're doing" (too generic) [20, 21]
*   "Bro" [20]

**4. Optimal Cold DM Length**
*   **Instagram DMs:** Keep it strictly under 100 words [17, 22]. The maximum acceptable length for initial outreach is 150 words (3-4 short sentences); anything over 200 words sees a 30% drop in response rates because mobile users skim [23, 24].
*   **LinkedIn DMs:** Keep messages short enough to fit inside LinkedIn's character limit without truncation [6].
*   **Cold Emails:** Keep them under 120 words (or under 100 words), totaling 3-5 sentences maximum [25-27].

**5. Direct Examples from Real Practitioners**

*   **Justin Welsh (Passive Founder DM):**
    > “Congrats on the recent round, {{FirstName}}. I'm a healthcare technology veteran (ZocDoc/PatientPop) and love what you're doing. Following you all from way over here in Los Angeles.” [1, 3]

*   **Jason Bay (Targeted Cold Email):**
    > "Hey Jason, Took a look at your LinkedIn post on cold calling. Loved your tip on going out to socialize at a coffee shop before making your cold calls. I'm an introvert, so found that helpful! Not sure if you run into this, but a challenge I've heard sales trainers talk about is finding reliable data. Especially if your clients are prospecting within LinkedIn. One way our ABC tool helps is by getting data from LinkedIn into your CRM in half the time. Would that be worth a few minutes of your time to chat about? John" [28]

*   **Becc Holland (Trigger-Aware Day 0 Email):**
    > "[Name] — saw [specific trigger] yesterday. Companies hitting that milestone in [segment] typically face [specific pain] inside 60 days. [Peer 1] and [Peer 2] solved it with [one-sentence outcome]. Worth comparing notes?" [29]

*   **Josh Braun (Laid-Back Anti-Pitch):**
    > "We show info product creators like yourself a lesser-known approach to reach salespeople who miss your posts on LinkedIn, ultimately driving more sales of your guide. Not sure if it's a fit, but I thought you might be interested." [30]
    *(Note: Braun's examples provided in the sources focus heavily on cold call scripts, such as opening with, "Hi I'm Josh. I was hoping you could help me out for a moment." [31])*

3. LinkedIn DM after connection accepted
Worth a 12-minute call next Tuesday to see if it lines up?
Cold Email Dynamics and Deliverability Thresholds
Sources: Woodpecker Cold Email Stats 2026, Lemlist Benchmark Report 2025, Instantly Deliverability Report 2026
6. Hyper-targeted DM campaigns achieve 18% conversion rates vs. 5% for generic broadcasts

Why it works: Owns the fact that it's a cold call and respects the prospect's time. Gong's analysis of 300M+ calls pegs this opener at roughly an 11.18% success rate Gong — the second-highest archetype in their dataset. Many prospects will say “Sure, go ahead,” which hands you the next 30 seconds. Follow up immediately with a reason for calling — don't waste the window you just earned.

The top performer, "How have you been?", functions as a powerful pattern interrupt on first-time calls.[18] The unexpected familiarity briefly scrambles the prospect's cognitive script, creating a window of attention for the caller.[18]

2. “How are you today?”
4. “I'm just calling to check in” / “I wanted to follow up”

There are numerous reasons these cold DM strategies don't work:

"Hey [Name], I've been following your work on [specific topic/content type] for a few weeks now, and I'm genuinely impressed by how you break down [specific insight they share]. Your recent post about [recent post reference] got me thinking—we work with creators who [specific value you provide], and I think there's a real opportunity here. No pressure, just curious if this is something worth exploring. Cheers, [Your Name]"
Problem: High CTR but low conversions
How long should my DM be?
7. Frequently Asked Questions
What to do instead:

What you need to make sure of at the bare minimum is that the first two sentences of a cold email sequence and that very first email are personalized and what you want to make sure is that this is something that's actually contextual. So don't make it completely unrelated to what you provide in your product or service, but you need to do enough there to make sure the prospect knows it's not clearly a mass email. So sure that you did your homework. If you're not personalizing beyond the first name and company name, the chances of getting a response go down dramatically. The “L” stands for “Laser focus”. So a couple of little quick tips here. When you're writing an email, you want to make sure that it's less than three to five sentences and also less than 120 words. So there's a couple of things that are recommended here. There are two tools. One is called Grammarly and the other is called Hemingway App and they're both free tools.
I'll also draw from my firsthand experience at Outbound Squad training reps at companies like Gong, Zoom, Shopify, and Rippling.
